reason: Class, component, object and deployment diagrams are all UML structure (structural) diagrams; behavioral diagrams are use case, activity, state, sequence, etc.
ER weak entities map to UML qualified aggregation/association, where the qualifier is the partial (discriminator) key; 'segregation' options are nonsense distractors.
Whole-part (object to its component parts) relationship in UML is aggregation.
A hash table implements an associative array: it maps keys to values, not values to keys.
Two or more keys hashing to the same bucket is by definition a collision.
Direct addressing allocates one distinct array slot for every possible key in the universe, so no hash function is needed.
Key doubles as the index, so lookup is a single array access: O(1).
A hash function maps a key to a slot index, i.e. computes the key's location in the array.
Randomized/uniform hash functions and chaining are all standard textbook collision-handling techniques, so 'all of the mentioned' is intended.
Load factor alpha = n/m, which under chaining is the average number of elements per chain (average chain length).
Simple uniform hashing assumes each element is equally likely to hash into any of the m slots, independently of others.
Expected search time under simple uniform hashing with chaining is Theta(1 + alpha), which is O(1) for constant load factor.
Doubly linked lists are the standard choice (CLRS) because deletion of a given element becomes O(1) rather than requiring a predecessor search.
The E-R diagram is the standard graphical representation of the database's logical structure.
A relationship set is drawn as a diamond; a double diamond is reserved for identifying relationship sets of weak entities.
In Silberschatz notation an entity set is a rectangle split into two parts: name on top, attributes below.
Arrows (directed lines) pointing to both entity sets mean 'at most one' on each side, i.e. one-to-one cardinality.
Roles are labels on the lines joining the relationship set (diamond) to the entity set (rectangle); the blanks are ordered diamond then rectangle.
By definition, an entity set lacking sufficient attributes for a primary key is a weak entity set.

Silberschatz calls it the identifying entity set; however 'owner set' (B) and 'strong entity set' (D) name the same thing, so the item has multiple defensible answers - flag as ambiguous even though A is the intended one.
